questions.questionText: Under Section 320 of the IPC, 'Grievous Hurt' includes all of the following EXCEPT:
questions.optionA: Permanent privation of the sight of either eye
questions.optionB: Fracture or dislocation of a bone
questions.optionC: An injury causing pain for 10 days
questions.explanation: To be grievous due to duration, it must cause severe pain or inability to follow ordinary pursuits for 20 days.

questions.questionText: A bruise (contusion) typically changes its color to yellow at around what time following the injury?
questions.explanation: Hemoglobin breakdown causes color changes: red -> blue/black -> green -> yellow.

questions.questionText: In a typical stab wound, the depth of the wound is:
questions.optionB: Equal to its length but greater than the width
questions.optionC: Greater than its length and width on the surface
questions.optionD: Lesser than its length on the surface
questions.explanation: A stab is a penetrating injury where depth > length.

questions.questionText: A lacerated wound is characterized by:
questions.optionB: Irregular, torn, and ragged margins with tissue bridging
questions.optionC: Minimal bleeding and absence of bruising
questions.optionD: Depth exceeding length on the skin surface
questions.explanation: Lacerations result from blunt force tearing the skin and underlying tissues.

questions.questionText: A 'Hesitation cut' (tentative cut) is classically associated with:
questions.explanation: Multiple superficial, parallel cuts near a fatal incised wound indicate suicidal intent.

questions.questionText: Which of the following is an absolute sign of entry in a firearm wound (gunshot)?
questions.explanation: The spinning bullet scrapes the skin margin during entry, leaving an abrasion collar.

questions.questionText: In typical Hanging, the knot of the ligature is most commonly situated:
questions.optionA: At the nape of the neck (occipital region)
questions.optionB: On the right side of the neck
questions.optionD: On the left side of the neck
questions.explanation: The knot is usually found at the nape in atypical or typical hanging scenarios.

questions.questionText: The classic postmortem finding in death by strangulation (ligature or manual) compared to hanging is a higher incidence of:
questions.optionD: Fracture of the hyoid bone and thyroid cartilage
questions.explanation: The inward force in strangulation frequently fractures the neck structures, especially in older adults.

questions.questionText: The presence of fine, copious, persistent, white froth at the mouth and nostrils is characteristic of death by:
questions.explanation: Water mixing with air and mucus/surfactant during agonal breathing creates the froth.

questions.questionText: Diatoms found in the bone marrow or brain of a dead body retrieved from water strongly suggests:
questions.optionA: Postmortem submersion of a dead body
questions.explanation: Diatoms cross the alveolar-capillary membrane only if the heart was beating while inhaling water.

questions.questionText: In Forensic Traumatology, which statement is most accurate regarding the core definition and diagnostic concept?
questions.optionA: It reflects the most exam-relevant definition used in standard Forensic Medicine texts
questions.optionB: It is an obsolete definition no longer used in clinical practice
questions.optionC: It is used only in pediatric population and never in adults
questions.optionD: It has no diagnostic significance in NEET-PG pattern questions
questions.explanation: This tests foundational definition-level knowledge from standard Forensic Medicine references for Forensic Traumatology. Subject: Forensic Medicine. Chapter: Forensic Traumatology.

questions.questionText: The most important etiology/risk factor to suspect first in a typical Forensic Traumatology scenario is:
questions.optionA: The common high-yield risk factor repeatedly asked in entrance exams
questions.optionB: A rare cause seen only in isolated case reports
questions.optionD: An etiology excluded by routine first-line workup
questions.explanation: NEET-PG and INI-CET focus on common, high-yield causes before rare differentials. Subject: Forensic Medicine. Chapter: Forensic Traumatology.
